text.title: API Versioning Strategies
text.summary: Strategy comparison, URL path versioning implementation, header versioning, and the additive-first rule.

rule.statement: New nullable fields, new optional inputs, and new endpoints are never breaking changes and never require a new version. Only create a new version for breaking semantic changes.
rule.rationale: 90% of API changes are additive. Version ceremony for additive changes wastes cycles and confuses consumers. Reserve versions for genuinely breaking changes: removed fields, changed types, new required inputs.

list.item: URL path (/api/v1/...) — cacheable, debuggable, explicit. Recommended default.
Header (Accept-Version: v1) — clean URLs, less visible, breaks CDN cache key-on-URL.
Query param (?version=1) — easy to add, poisons CDN cache keys.
Content-Type negotiation — standards-based but complex client-side handling.

rule.statement: Default to URL path versioning. Never use query-param versioning for cacheable endpoints.
rule.rationale: URL path versioning is the boring, CDN-friendly, browser-debuggable choice. Every major public API (Stripe, Twilio, GitHub) uses it.

example.note: v1 routes are frozen. v2 lives in a separate module the agent cannot edit when working on v1.

example.code: GET /users HTTP/1.1 Host: api.example.com Accept-Version: v2
example.note: Header versioning keeps URLs clean but requires CDN configuration to vary cache on the header.
